title: "Il liberalismo nel XXI secolo" convegno internazionale promosso dalla Fondazione "Amici di Liberal" con il patrocinio del Comune di Napoli (Napoli, 4-7 giugno 1997)
text: Non è una definizione, ma una regola, che dice che si può chiamare un paese una democrazia quando ha cambiato il suo governo due volte per le elezioni. Una volta non è abbastanza. Abbiamo visto che un governo in potere, sia in principio, sia in advertenza, può essere votato senza potere. E quelli che vengono a rimanere il paese, prendono una buona cura di non partire dalla stessa rotta in cui erano venuti. Troppo, credo, che lo stabiliscono abbastanza bene. La democrazia, in questo senso in cui ho definito, ha una relazionazione molto limitata in storia umana e geografia. In base, la forma di governo ha funzionato in una regione comparativamente piccola del mondo, principalmente negli paesi inglesi. E' stato trasplantato con successo intermitente al continento dell'Europa, dove il record è, diciamo, mixato, e i prospetti anche. Con in mente le vicissitudini della democrazia europea, dovremmo essere più tolleranti delle difficoltà che sono currentemente incontrate da quelli che stanno cercando di introdurre la democrazia stile occidentale in circostanti ancora più infamili. Forse potrei fare il mio punto più chiaro con una semplice analogia introdotta da un'altra invenzione della stessa fonte, il gioco del football. Il football è un gioco in cui due parti contengono e fanno il loro piacere di vincere, ma entrambi hanno gli stessi diritti, entrambi operano sotto le stesse regole, entrambi sanno che neanche vittoria e non vincere è finale, e si incontrano per un piatto dopo. Sì, forse uno potrebbe fare un'altra cosa, che, anche se ci possono essere molti team, il gioco è più efficace quando ci sono solo due nel campo allo stesso tempo. Andiamo a un altro termine importante, che, di nuovo, è stato usato un numero di volte nelle nostre discussioni qui, il termine fondamentalismo. Questo è un termine, la storia della quale è conosciuta, il significato originale della quale è molto specifico. Il nome fondamentalismo, il vero fondamentalismo, non è soltanto esterno, è americano-protestante. È stato usato prima, secondo le lexicografie, in circa il 1920, per designare alcune strade americane-protestanti e per differenziarli dalla strada principale delle strade protestanti. Il nome viene da una serie di bokletti pubblicati da queste strade, da un clergyman da queste strade, con la lettera generale, i fondamentali. Una serie di bokletti chiamati fondamentali, quindi i fondamentalisti, quelli che credono su queste principali. I fondamentalisti differono da la strada principale delle strade protestanti e, dunque, altri cristiani, su due punti maggiori. La teologia liberale e il critico biblico. Li hanno obbligato fortemente a entrambi. Erano contro la teologia liberale in tutte le forme e insistivano sulle textuali divinità e inerranza della Bibbia. Ora, in Irlanda, sono stato accostumato a usare questo termo fondamentalismo con una lunga range di applicazioni. E può essere mislevo. Sto parlando specificamente di quello chiamato il fondamentalismo islamico. La teologia liberale è stata un'issue nel passato tra gli islami. Può essere ancora un'issue nel futuro tra gli islami. Non è un'issue nel momento presente. Non è quello a cui stanno parlando. Per l'inerranza e la divinità della Bibbia è una dogma basica. La divinità del Coran, una che è riconosciuta, o almeno non è chiamata da tutti gli islami, nonostante per il loro credere, il loro praticare, o nemmeno acquiescente. La differenza tra quelli che noi oggi chiamiamo fondamentali islamici e altri islami è qualcosa di molto diverso. È la domanda, basicamente, della natura islamica del Stato. È una politica e, a un certo punto, una differenza sociale. Il loro movimento è una protesta contro ciò che vedono come la deislamizzazione del Stato e della società nei paesi islamici sotto le influenze perniciose, alienanze e infideli. E il loro rimedio è la reislamizzazione del Stato e della società, in particolare, con la reimposazione dell'Islamo Sospetto, della Sharia, che gli islami credono diventare divinissimi e ottenere. Alcuni hanno protestato contro l'uso di questo termine, in particolare, perché ha avuto un elemento di pregiudizio,  in particolare, perché ha avuto un elemento di pregiudizio, di condemnation. In alcuni paesi, il termine islamismo è usato e quelli che professano queste creazioni sono chiamati islamisti. Questo, a me sembra, è molto più pericoloso, perché avrebbe impiato che questo sia qualcosa di centrale e tipico dell'islam, che non è certamente. Nel francese, il termine integrismo è usato, in italiano dico integrismo. Queste sono di nuovo termini cristiani westerni, irrelevanti all'islam, ma non voglio pronunciare in francese o in italiano. Ciò che mi sembra significativo è che in anni recenti il termine inglese, il termine americano-protestante, il termine fondamentalismo è stato letteralmente, textualmente traslato in arabic e persiano e turco e, non dimenticare, in altre lingue dell'islam e usato per designare questi movimenti. E se gli islamisti usano questo termine per designare i movimenti islamici, penso che avremo bisogno di fare la stessa cosa e non sentire l'imbarizio. La mia quarta definizione è la termina islamica. È già stata menzionata molte volte in questo meeting, e ancora le certe ambiguità che arrivano. Per iniziare, c'è una cosa molto ovvia, ovvia quando è menzionata, ma non molte volte osservata, che è che usiamo in inglese e in altre lingue, abbiamo già usato il termine islamo in due sensi diversi. Lo usiamo come l'equivalente della cristianità, cioè il nome di una religione, e lo usiamo anche come l'equivalente del cristianismo, cioè di una civilizzazione, una cultura storica, un fenomeno, una civilizzazione che è cresciuta sotto l'egis di quella religione. Non è necessario di spiegare a questa audience la differenza nel significato, nel scopo tra i due termini cristianità e cristianità, è più difficile spiegare e mantenere la differenza tra questi due differenti definizioni o usaggi del stesso termine islamo. L'ultimo professore Marshall Hodgson della Università di Chicago, un grande scolaro su la storia islamica e la civilizzazione, che ha detto questa distinzione, ha suggerito che useremo il termine islamdom come l'equivalente del cristianismo, una buona suggerizione, ma non ha mai accaduto, e nessuno, inoltre i personali disibili del prof. Hodgson hanno mai usato questo termine in inglese, non so quale l'equivalente in altre lingue, esattamente in turca, dove ci sono due parole da distinguere, ma non in arabica e altre lingue. È molto difficile generalizzare l'islamo. Stiamo parlando, ricordate, di più di 14 secoli di storia. Stiamo parlando di più di un milione di persone. Stiamo parlando di 54 stati sovrapposti, membri dell'organizzazione dell'Islamo Congresso, più grandi minorità musulmane in Cina, in India, e ora in crescendo in l'Europa e le America. Inoltre, bisogna fare l'attento. Let me turn now to the relationship between religion and political culture and try to establish some ground rules which I will follow in my discussion of Islam and Islam's relationship to liberalism, to democracy, and more generally to political culture. In a sense, the question is Islam compatible with liberal democracy is part of a larger question. Is religion compatible with liberal democracy? Here I think it may be useful to make a basic distinction which applies to all religions. The distinction between religion in the sense of belief, conduct, worship, and religion in the sense of an ideology of empowerment and enforcement. In this first sense, religion as a system of belief, of conduct, and of worship, in that sense there seems to me to be no reason why any religion in those terms should be incompatible with liberal democracy, nor indeed with any form of government that is not in itself inherently evil. Such have existed as we know, as we were recently reminded. In the other sense, religion as an ideology of empowerment, that of course is another matter. I think it may be useful to point to what seems to me to be the main differences between the Christian Jewish and Islamic approaches. And I would like to do so in terms of what, without intending any disrespect, I would call the founding myths of the three religions. Moses, you will recall, was not permitted to enter the promised land. And the Hebrew Bible, the Old Testament, is dominated by the two themes of bondage and liberation of exile and return. Jesus was crucified and his followers survived as a persecuted minority for centuries before finally converting the state and inaugurating the processes of the Christianization of Rome, and some would add the Romanization of Christ. The founding story of Islam is very different. The Prophet Muhammad conquered his promised land and established a state, a polity of which he was chief. Not he, but some of his enemies were put to death. And his scripture tells the story first of a struggle to attain power, and then of the exercise of power. Muhammad created the first Muslim state in Medina and he was the head of it. And he did what heads of state do. He dispensed he promulgated laws, he dispensed justice, he collected taxes, he made war, he made peace. In other words, he acted as a political sovereign. And the the record, the memories of his sovereign acts as well as of his prophetic mission are part of the central core of self awareness of Muslims everywhere in the world. And that I think is a very very important, indeed a crucial difference in the Muslim attitude to politics in general and to the conduct of government in particular. A subject which I should add is much discussed in Muslim religious and legal literature. There is a Muslim political culture. Indeed, one may speak of several Muslim political cultures. If one wants to look at the compatibility of Christianity and democracy, one has I think to take three or perhaps more accurately four different varieties. Again, I am not concerned with theological or philosophical or theoretical statements of any kind on this particular point, merely with the historical record. If you look at the historical record of the attempts to run democratic societies in countries of Orthodox, of Catholic and of Protestant Christianity, you would I think be able to draw up an interesting comparative table. I said four rather than three. The fourth I would call the post-Christian society. I would remind you that secularism is a Christian notion. Secularism is a post-Christian answer to a Christian dilemma, one which is posed in the Gospels, the dilemma of God and Caesar and which continued to haunt Christians throughout the history of Christianity. That particular dilemma was not a problem for Muslims. The very notion of secularism, of laity, of totally alien. Arabic is a Christian as well as a Muslim language. There are large words, large and important Christian communities of Arabic speech and a considerable Arabic Christian literature. So the Arabs did have an advantage over the Persians and Turks and other peoples in that they had a Christian vocabulary available to them in their language. So there was a word for secular in Arabic. There was none in Persian or Turkish. They had to invent them. Laity is again an alien notion. The idea that some things are clerical, others are lay is a Christian notion of very little relevance. A point I think is very important to emphasize is that these three religions, Judaism, Christianity, Islam, are closely related. If one compares them with the great religions of Asia, with Buddhism, Hinduism, the religions of the Far East, they are as alike as peas in a pod. From a larger perspective, one would I say, one might I say regard these as variants of the same basic religious tradition. That is I think nowadays partly at least recognized. Several speakers in the last couple of days have used the term the Judeo-Christian tradition or Judeo-Christian religion. This is an old reality but a very modern term. Before the present century, it would have been found offensive on both sides of the hyphen. Nowadays it's passed into common usage on both sides of the hyphen. One could of course though one doesn't usually speak with equal justification of a Judeo-Islamic tradition. Jewish life in the period between the destruction of the ancient Jewish polity and the creation of the modern Jewish polity was passed mainly in the lands of Christendom and of Islam. The Jewish experience outside these two is insignificant. And there is a Judeo-Islamic tradition just as there is a Judeo-Christian tradition. One of the more interesting things about the modern state of Israel is that therefore the first time in many centuries the Judeo-Christian and the Judeo-Islamic traditions meet in the same state and one sees the great differences between them almost a laboratory controlled experiment on the larger encounter of Christendom and Islam. This kinship between Christianity and Islam and Judaism has made communication possible. We find for example in the Middle Ages in Spain, in Sicily and elsewhere Christians and Muslims engaging in disputations, theological disputations. This was possible because both were speaking the same language figuratively as well as literally talking about the same things. Such a disputation would have made impossible with a Hindu or a Buddhist or a Confucian, there would have been no common language. When Christians and Muslims denounced each other as infidels each knew exactly what they meant because they meant the same thing. When Christians and Muslims said to each other you are an unbeliever and you will burn in hell, again both understood what the other meant because both meant precisely the same thing. Their heavens are somewhat different but their hells are almost identical. All that by way of introduction to explain some of the terms that I am using. I want to turn now to a more specific examination of Islam in relation to liberalism, democracy, liberal democracy, whatever and to consider first those elements in Islamic civilization, in Islamic history which would militate against the success of democracy in Islamic lands and then since I prefer to end on an optimistic note then those that are more positive and more favourable. The first and most obvious one is the unmistakable authoritarianism of the Islamic political tradition. An authoritarianism which it shares of course with most of Christendom for most of Christian history but which in the case of Islam is almost completely unrelieved. This authoritarianism is both historic and doctrinal. If we look at the record of Muslim states we find that authoritarian rule is the norm. If we look at the 54 Muslim states at the present time the vast majority are authoritarian in one form or another. Only one of them remotely qualifies as a democracy by Huntington's definition of having changed the government twice by elections, that is the Turkish Republic and that is still a very troubled democracy, although it does change governments by elections. There is also a doctrinal authoritarianism in the very notion of holy law, of a divine law, revealed, formulated, promulgated which regulates not only those things which in other religions might be regarded as specifically religious but also every aspect of life and more particularly such things as marriage, divorce, inheritance, family life and the rest and of course the conduct of the state. That is not very conducive to the development of liberal institutions or democratic ways. The earliest description of England that we have, the earliest that is to say in any detail, is a Muslim from Lucknow who visited England in 1798. He was of Persian background and wrote a book in Persian a fascinating account of England and France and one of the places his hosts took him to was the House of Commons and this he tries to explain to his readers back home. He begins with a rather unflattering comparison. He describes the two sides of the house and says this is like what one often sees in India two trees on opposite sides of the road both full of parrots squawking at each other. But then he goes on to explain what this is for and he explains that the poor benighted English unlike the Muslims had never accepted a divine law and were therefore reduced to the expedient of making their own laws in accordance with the experience of their judges and the necessities of the moment, which he regards obviously as a very poor expedient the people who have denied themselves this major benefit of divine guidance. That system you don't need a legislative assembly and that of course has far reaching implications. Another factor against linked to this is the triumphalism. The belief which Islam shares with Christianity and as far as I know with no other religion are being the final possessor of God's truth which it is therefore their sacred duty to bring to others. This again makes the interchange and exchange of democratic life more difficult. One of the most serious obstructions is the position of women. The first who raised this as a major issue as far as I am aware in a Muslim country was Kamal Atatürk who again has been mentioned several times at these meetings, the founder and first president of the Turkish Republic, an Ottoman Pasha and a military general, an improbable feminist. Nevertheless he was the first to proclaim the need for the emancipation of women and the giving of political rights to women. He said several times in a series of speeches which he delivered in Anatolia in the early 1920s, he said that our present task is to catch up with the modern world. We will not catch up with the modern world if we only modernize half the population. A point which he understood which many others still have difficulty in understanding. If we look at the accounts of the West written by Muslim travelers this is the point on which almost all seem to agree the astonishing to them freedom accorded to women in Western and Christian society. Obviously the position of women was very far from one of equality in the West but it was incomparably better than in most other parts of the world. One that I find particularly instructive is a report from a Moroccan ambassador in Spain in the late 17th century who comments on the free and easy ways of Spanish ladies and the lack of manly jealousy of Spanish men. This is Spain of all places in the late 17th century. His report on Spain doesn't tell us much about Spain but he tells us quite a lot about Morocco. One of the major reasons for the relative backwardness of Islam is surely this point. If a society persists in depriving itself of the talents and services of half the population and of entrusting the nurture of the other half to uneducated and down trodden mothers, it will not be able to keep up with the modern world nor will it be able to operate any kind of free liberal democratic society. This is increasingly understood not always not only I should say by women, there are there as elsewhere some women who prefer subordination but I feel myself that women who are after all slightly more than half the population may yet constitute the best hope for liberal democracy in the Islamic world. I have already mentioned the lack of a legislative power and therefore the absence of any need for a legislative assembly. One could add to that the difficulties arising, this may seem to you an odd formulation but I stand by it, difficulties arising at the present time from oil. Oil has enormously enriched some Muslim governments. It has freed them from the need to raise taxes. It has freed them therefore from the need to enter into a dialogue with their subjects. It has provided resources to maintain tyranny at home and terrorism abroad and in the long run it may well prove to be a great curse rather than a blessing. And it is surely not without significance that the most hopeful signs of development towards a freer society occur in countries like Tunisia and Jordan and Turkey which have no oil. Another difficulty is the absence in Muslim law of what in Roman law is called the legal person. There are only individuals. There is no city, just an accumulation of quarters and houses. There is no abstract legal, no corporate legal entity. This is an alien notion. Therefore no form of representation, no form of majority decision. In the past this didn't matter but in trying to create liberal democratic institutions the absence of such a tradition is a serious obstacle. It's an obstacle which is now increasingly being overcome as the modern economy has created the need for such institutions and such laws. Let me turn now to what seem to me to be the factors in Islam which are conducive to the development of liberal democracy. The first and most important of these of the political factors is the Muslim doctrine of sovereignty. This is a subject which is widely discussed by jurists and theologians in Islam going back a very long way. And the supreme institution of sovereignty is the caliphate and there is much discussion as to how one becomes caliph and what one's duties are. And a reading of these texts makes it quite clear that according to Muslim doctrine the supreme sovereign office is contractual and consensual. The caliph becomes caliph by a contract between him and his subjects. A contract which imposes duties as well as giving rights to the ruler. And it is consensual in that it depends on the consent of the ruled. Now you might say this is theoretical, this isn't how it actually happened. This would be a valid, though not entirely valid criticism. In that generally speaking certain minimal norms were observed. It is rare in Islamic history that you find a complete despotism. You find authoritarian rule certainly, but not unprincipled, unrestricted despotic rule. It is true that the law gives authoritarian, gives autocratic powers to the ruler, but it nevertheless defines those powers. An oft cited tradition of the prophet, as well as the Qur'an, Muslims believe in the traditions of the prophet, these are sayings of the prophet handed down by oral tradition. A much quoted tradition of the prophet is there is no obedience in sin. That means that the duty of obedience which the subject owes to the ruler lapses when the ruler orders something that is sinful. And in that case duty of obedience is replaced not by a right of disobedience as in western political thought, but by a duty of disobedience. Of course, there is no constitutional procedure to decide when a command is sinful. That is left to what we might tactfully call the play of historical forces. But the notion is there, the principle is there and it has increasingly been cited in modern times by Muslim dissidents of various kinds. Some reference was made this morning to tolerance. Professor Baudet spoke very rightly of the inadequacy of tolerance as a notion in our time. There is a letter of George Washington addressed to the leader of the Jewish community in Newport, Rhode Island, in which he says, I'm quoting from memory but I think accurately, he says, let us no longer speak of toleration as if it were by the indulgence of one group of people that others enjoy the exercise of their inherent natural rights. That is just over 200 years ago. It is still not generally accepted. In saying that though, I think we should bear in mind the very real tolerance exercised by Muslim governments at a time when this was very far from being the norm. The evolution of tolerance in Christian societies was slow, difficult, and reluctant. Christian states for many centuries found it difficult to tolerate not only other religions but even deviant forms of their own. In the medieval Muslim states on the other hand, there was an acceptance of diversity. The Quran itself says there is no constraint in religion. A German scholar has recently argued that this is not a plea for tolerance but an expression of resignation. That is not how it was generally understood. There is also a saying attributed to the prophet, difference of opinion among my people is a blessing from God. Again, an argument that diversity, that pluralism are good things in themselves. This tolerance was of course limited. It did not mean as some modern apologists would have it the granting of equal rights of citizenship to non-Muslims. That would have been seen not as a merit but as a dereliction of duty. It is obviously absurd to give equal rights to those who reject it. Nevertheless, there was a very real tolerance allowed to non-Muslim communities and even a much more searching test, even deviant Muslim communities. One does not find heresy hunts and inquisitions in Islamic history except on very rare and exceptional circumstances. In most of the Muslim states, Muslims of different branches of the faith, Christians of different churches, Jews lived side by side. Now, this was limited tolerance, but it was much more extensive in one sense than what is accorded to minorities of the present time. And this gives rise to one of the difficulties of the accommodation of Muslim minorities in Christian or post-Christian states in Europe and elsewhere at the present time. Muslims naturally expect the same tolerance from Christian governments as they in the past gave to Christian minorities in Muslim countries. What they are in fact getting is much more than that but also much less than that. The status of a tolerated minority under Muslim rule carried with it a very large measure of internal autonomy. The Christian Ecclesiastical Authorities had real and enforceable jurisdiction over the faithful. They administered their own laws and adjudicated cases in their own tribunals. The same was true of the Jews. So that in the same street if you had, shall we say, a Muslim, an Orthodox Christian, a Catholic Christian and a Jew, they would be subject to four different systems of law. If they died, their estates would be divided according to four different systems of inheritance law and so on and so on. Even to some extent criminal jurisdiction. Let me quote one example and an important one. The question of polygamy and concubinage. Muslim law permits both polygamy and concubinage. Jewish law in the Islamic lands, though not in the Christian lands, permits polygamy but not concubinage. Christian law of all churches at all times prohibits both polygamy and concubinage. It was therefore possible for rabbinic and Christian church authorities to punish people and sometimes punish them severely for offenses which were not offenses at all according to the law of the land, the dominant community in the land or breaches of the marriage laws. They controlled their own educational systems. They had their own schools in which they taught whatever they wanted to teach, provided only that it was not subversive and even that was not very rigorously enforced. One understands then that Muslims who come to Europe have a different notion of minority rights and minority status and a different expectation. These minorities in principle had no political rights. They were not supposed to participate in any way in the conduct of government. They were, with insignificant exceptions, excluded from the bearing of arms or from any form of military service. In fact they did sometimes participate in politics but always in a relatively subordinate capacity. Here, the other side. This degree of tolerance is of course no longer acceptable by modern standards and it broke down in the 19th century when the non-Muslim subjects of the Ottoman Empire and of other Muslim states were no longer satisfied with the tolerated and subordinate status accorded to them by the old laws. They demanded emancipation, they demanded equal rights and so on. The old system broke down. It has not been adequately replaced. Nevertheless, I think one should remember that for a long time the Muslim world was far more tolerant than Christian countries. Nowadays we tend to measure tolerance by the movement of refugees and intolerance rather by the movement of refugees from persecuting authorities to free authorities. We should bear in mind that until the 16th and 17th centuries the movement of refugees was almost entirely isolated from Christendom to Islam, not only of Jews but even of deviant Christians. In 16th and 17th century Istanbul, for example, there were Protestants from Catholic countries, Catholics from Protestant countries and Jews from both enjoying the relatively free air of the Turkish Muslim capital. The economic point is again I think an important one. We tend nowadays to think of the Islamic world as irretrievably feudal and therefore opposed to free market and other forms of capitalist enterprise. This is a reversal of the historical sequence. In the West the classical sequence is of course slavery, feudalism, capitalism. But the Islamic history is not Marxist. It doesn't follow the Marxist rule. It is of course slavery, feudalism, capitalism. But the Islamic history is not Marxist. It doesn't follow the Marxist rule. These matters were debated intensely and we have expressions of different views. The question, for example, of price fixing versus market forces was hotly debated rather      than a thousand years ago in Baghdad and Damascus and other cities of the Middle East. They quote a saying attributed to the prophet, only God can fix prices. An eloquent theological formulation of laisse faire. Another saying is that he who earns money is the friend of God. Another saying the honest merchant who is killed is a martyr. In other words, counts as a martyr for the faith. The importance of the commercial development of medieval Islam is recorded even by the vocabulary which we use in European languages at a time when feudal Europe was a rather impoverished primitive outpost on the wild northwest frontier of the Islamic world. Think, for example, of how many Muslim words, how many Arabic or Persian words there are in our commercial vocabulary. Tariff is Arabic. The Italian dogana, the French douane, come from the Spanish adwana which come from the perso Arabic diwan. Czech is Persian and one could add many others which came from a financially and economically more advanced society to a more primitive one. The change there, as I said, reversed the Marxist sequence, a point which Marx seems to have overlooked. With uncharacteristic humility, he did recognize that the canonical western sequence didn't apply to other societies and he met that by inventing the Asiatic mode of production which presumably covers everything from Anatolia to Japan. The feudal age of the Islamic economy has lasted for a very long time but there again there is an earlier tradition to which one can look back with, I think, elements conducive to a better development.
